Reverse Engineering — Auth & Credentials

How to log into things, get API keys, and store credentials — for any web platform.

In this section

Section titled “In this section”
NextAuth.js / Auth.js Session cookies, CSRF double-submit, JWT vs database sessions.
WorkOS SSO flows, session cookies, API key provisioning.
macOS Keychain Reading credentials written by desktop apps via the Security framework.

How web auth works

Section titled “How web auth works”

Every web login — from a 2005 PHP app to a 2026 Next.js SPA — does the same three things:

  1. You prove who you are (type a password, click a link, enter a code)
  2. The server gives you a cookie (session token, JWT, whatever)
  3. You send that cookie with every request

That’s it. The mechanism varies — form POSTs, fetch calls, OAuth redirects — but the end result is always a cookie in your browser.

The two submission patterns

Section titled “The two submission patterns”

When you click “Submit” on a login form, one of two things happens:

Form POST (the classic). The browser sends an HTML form POST, the server responds with a redirect (302), and the browser follows it. Cookies get set along the way. This is the oldest pattern on the web and still used everywhere, including modern frameworks like NextAuth.

Fetch/XHR (the SPA way). JavaScript makes an async request, the page stays loaded, and the response is handled in JS. The page might update without a full navigation.

Both are straightforward. When reverse engineering, you just need to figure out which one a site uses, then replay it.

Cookies

Section titled “Cookies”

A cookie is a name-value pair the server sends with Set-Cookie and the browser sends back with every request. The attributes control where and how:

AttributeWhat it meansHTTP client impact
HttpOnlyJS can’t read itDoesn’t affect agentos.http (only matters in browsers)
SecureHTTPS onlyUse https:// URLs
SameSite=LaxSent on navigations, not cross-site POSTsagentos.http sends it normally
Domain=.example.comWorks on all subdomainsImportant when auth and dashboard are on different subdomains. The engine uses RFC 6265 domain matching to filter cookies by host from connection.base_url
ExpirySession (until browser close) or persistent (date)agentos.http doesn’t care — just send the cookie

Cross-domain cookies: When auth lives at auth.exa.ai and the dashboard at dashboard.exa.ai, the session cookie is scoped to .exa.ai so both subdomains can use it. When extracting cookies, always check the domain — .exa.ai works everywhere, auth.exa.ai only works on auth.

CSRF tokens

Section titled “CSRF tokens”

Sites protect against forged requests by requiring a CSRF token — a secret value the server generates and the client must include in form submissions.

The pattern is always the same:

  1. Fetch the token (from an endpoint, a meta tag, a hidden form field, or a cookie)
  2. Include it in your POST (as a form field, header, or both)

The token and cookie must come from the same request. If you fetch the token with one HTTPX client and try to use it with another, the server will reject it because the CSRF cookie doesn’t match.

Where to find CSRF tokens during discovery:

The credential bootstrap

Section titled “The credential bootstrap”

This is the end-to-end flow for getting credentials from a web dashboard. Every dashboard app follows these five steps.

1. Navigate to the dashboard

Section titled “1. Navigate to the dashboard”

Go to the dashboard URL (not the auth URL directly). The dashboard redirects to auth with the right callback URL.

If it lands on a Cloudflare challenge page, that’s fine — the Playwright browser solves it automatically and you get a cf_clearance cookie.

2. Figure out how to log in

Section titled “2. Figure out how to log in”

Check what login methods are available:

Inspect the form:

This tells you:

  • Email + code → usually fully replayable with agentos.http (see below)
  • Email + password → replay entirely with agentos.http
  • Google/GitHub OAuth → Playwright for the consent screen, then cookies
  • SSO (WorkOS, Okta) → see vendor guides

3. Complete the login

Section titled “3. Complete the login”

Try agentos.http first. Many email+code flows that appear browser-only are actually fully replayable. The key technique is scanning the JS bundles for custom verification endpoints (e.g. /api/verify-otp) and using the Navigation API interceptor to discover token formats. See Discovery: JS Bundle Scanning and Discovery: Navigation API Interception.

Fall back to Playwright only for flows that genuinely require a browser (Google OAuth consent screens, CAPTCHAs, or complex multi-step redirects). Use type (not fill) for input fields on React forms.

If the login involves a verification code from email, the agent checks email between steps.

4. Grab the cookies

Section titled “4. Grab the cookies”

You want the session cookie (usually next-auth.session-token, session, auth_token, etc.) and optionally cf_clearance for Cloudflare.

Validate it works:

5. Hit the dashboard APIs

Section titled “5. Hit the dashboard APIs”

Navigate to the API keys page and capture what the frontend calls:

This typically reveals endpoints for:

  • Listing API keys
  • Team/org info (rate limits, billing, usage)
  • User profile

Always read the full API response. Dashboards mask values in the UI (showing 9d2e4b••••••) but the API often returns them in full. Exa’s /api/get-api-keys returns the complete API key as the id field — the UI masking is purely client-side.

6. Store credentials

Section titled “6. Store credentials”

Return them via __secrets__ so the engine stores them securely:

The engine writes to the vault, creates an account entity on the graph, and strips __secrets__ before the response reaches the agent.

Observing network traffic

Section titled “Observing network traffic”

Three tools, each for a different situation.

capture_network — what the page calls on load

Section titled “capture_network — what the page calls on load”

Navigate to a URL and record all fetch/XHR traffic for a few seconds.

Use this to discover dashboard APIs, auth endpoints, and data shapes. Good patterns to filter with:

Fetch interceptor — what a button click triggers

Section titled “Fetch interceptor — what a button click triggers”

When you need to see what happens after a user interaction (like clicking “Create Key”), inject this before clicking:

Form inspection — what a form POST sends

Section titled “Form inspection — what a form POST sends”

If the fetch interceptor captures nothing but the browser navigated somewhere new, the form did a native POST (full page navigation). Just inspect the form to see what it sends:

This gives you the action URL, the method, and all input fields including hidden ones (CSRF tokens, honeypots).

After the form submits, the browser lands on a new page. Check where you ended up (url) and grab the cookies (cookies { domain: "..." }). That’s all you need — the form POST did its job and set the session cookies.

Quick reference

Section titled “Quick reference”

Replaying with agentos.client

Section titled “Replaying with agentos.client”

Once you understand what the browser does, replay it with agentos.client. The goal is to get the same cookies without a browser.

Apps use agentos.client for all HTTP — never raw httpx/requests/urllib. Browser-grade headers (UA, Sec-CH-UA*, Sec-Fetch-*) come from the connection’s client= value; the ambient Jar carries cookies. Python controls caller headers per request via headers={...}; the engine’s TLS fingerprint (Chrome via wreq+BoringSSL) is a fixed match to the Python-side client hints.

Form POSTs

Section titled “Form POSTs”

Fetch/XHR calls

Section titled “Fetch/XHR calls”

Picking a client= kind

Section titled “Picking a client= kind”
KindBundleUse for
"browser"UA + full nav headers (Sec-Fetch-Dest: document, Upgrade-Insecure-Requests, Accept: text/html,…)Form POSTs, page loads, top-level nav
"fetch"UA + XHR headers (Sec-Fetch-Mode: cors, Accept: application/json)AJAX/fetch calls from within a browser page
"api" (default)No bundle — caller provides everythingBare REST with key/token auth

Per-request tweaks (headers={...} merges on top; http2=False for Vercel JA4; incognito=True to bypass the Jar; skip_cookies=[...] to drop specific cookies from the outbound header).

When replay doesn’t work

Section titled “When replay doesn’t work”

Sometimes the server does something specific to browser requests that agentos.client can’t replicate (custom redirect handling, Cloudflare challenges, JS-dependent cookie setting). When that happens:

  1. Use Playwright for that step. Let the browser handle it.
  2. Extract the cookies from Playwright after.
  3. Use agentos.client for everything else (dashboard APIs, data extraction, etc.).

This isn’t a workaround — it’s the right architecture. Playwright handles the login, agentos.client handles the work. Each tool does what it’s good at.

SituationSolution
Standard form POST or API callagentos.client replay with client="browser" or "fetch"
Custom OTP/code verificationScan JS bundles for custom endpoints → agentos.client replay (see discovery)
Google OAuth consent screenPlaywright first login → cookies → agentos.client after
Cloudflare JS challengePlaywright or brave-browser.cookie_get for cf_clearance
Vercel Security Checkpoint (429)client.get(url, http2=False) — purely a JA4 fingerprint issue
CAPTCHACookies from user’s real browser session
Unknown client-side token constructionNavigation API interceptor → read the actual URL (see discovery)

Cookie injection from real browsers

Section titled “Cookie injection from real browsers”

The fastest way to do authenticated discovery. When the user is already logged into a site in Brave/Firefox, skip the login flow entirely — extract cookies from their real browser and inject them into Playwright or agentos.http.

The pattern

Section titled “The pattern”

Why this matters

Section titled “Why this matters”
  • No login flow needed. The user is already logged in. Don’t waste time reverse-engineering auth when you just need to see what a page looks like.
  • Real session state. You get the exact cookies the browser has — including HttpOnly cookies, auth tokens, and CSRF state that would be hard to reproduce.
  • Playwright stays authenticated. After injecting cookies into capture_network or goto, the Playwright browser session keeps them. Subsequent click, fill, inspect calls stay logged in.

The ambient Jar handles multi-step flows automatically

Section titled “The ambient Jar handles multi-step flows automatically”

When making multi-step requests (e.g., fetch a form page, then submit it), the connection’s ambient Jar carries Set-Cookie responses across calls for free. No explicit jar config, no cookie header threading.

Compare to the old (now-impossible) anti-pattern: threading a raw Cookie: header through every call manually, so step 1’s new _session_id2 never rides on step 2. The Jar makes that class of bug unrepresentable.

Available cookie providers

Section titled “Available cookie providers”
ProviderToolNotes
brave-browsercookie_get({ domain: "..." })Decrypts from Brave’s encrypted cookie DB
firefoxcookie_get({ domain: "..." })Reads from Firefox profile
playwrightcookie_get({ domain: "..." })From Playwright’s own browser session (after login)

Working with Playwright

Section titled “Working with Playwright”

Practical notes for driving Playwright during discovery.

Use type, not fill, for React forms

Section titled “Use type, not fill, for React forms”

React manages input state through synthetic events. fill sets the DOM value directly, bypassing React — the component state stays empty and submit buttons stay disabled. type sends real keystrokes that trigger onChange handlers.

If the submit button is disabled after entering text, you probably need type.

Watch for honeypot fields

Section titled “Watch for honeypot fields”

Some login forms have hidden inputs designed to catch bots:

These are invisible to users but bots that fill every field get caught. In HTTPX replay, never include these fields. Common names: website, url, homepage, company, fax.

If your HTTPX replay silently fails (200 response but nothing happens), check for honeypot fields you might be filling.

Navigate to dashboard, not auth

Section titled “Navigate to dashboard, not auth”

Always start at the dashboard URL. The auth domain needs the callbackUrl parameter (set by the dashboard redirect) to know where to send you after login. Going to auth directly often shows “accessed incorrectly” errors.

Clearing state for a fresh run

Section titled “Clearing state for a fresh run”

Useful when existing cookies skip you past the login page and you need to observe the full flow from scratch.

Auth patterns

Section titled “Auth patterns”

NextAuth.js / Auth.js

Section titled “NextAuth.js / Auth.js”

The most common pattern for Next.js dashboards. Recognized by /api/auth/* endpoints and next-auth.* cookies.

Quick identification:

  • GET /api/auth/csrf returns a CSRF token
  • GET /api/auth/providers lists available login methods
  • Session cookie: next-auth.session-token (encrypted JWT, ~30 day expiry)

Email login flow (fully HTTPX for custom OTP sites):

  1. GET /api/auth/csrf → CSRF token (HTTPX)
  2. POST /api/auth/signin/email → triggers email (HTTPX)
  3. POST /api/verify-otp → verify code, get token components (HTTPX)
  4. GET /api/auth/callback/email?token=... → session cookie set (HTTPX)

The key insight: many NextAuth sites with custom OTP code entry have a hidden /api/verify-otp endpoint discoverable via JS bundle scanning. The callback token format (hashedOtp:rawOtp) was discovered using the Navigation API interceptor. See nextauth.md for the full deep dive. Reference implementation: apps/web/exa/.

AWS Cognito

Section titled “AWS Cognito”

Common in gym/fitness SaaS (Approach, Mindbody, etc.). Pure AWS API calls — no browser needed at all.

Find the ClientId in the app’s JS bundle — search for userPoolId or userPoolClientId.

WorkOS

Section titled “WorkOS”

B2B auth platform. Supports SSO, social login, and email. Recognized by workos_id in JWT claims.

See workos.md for the full deep dive.

Cookie-based sessions (generic)

Section titled “Cookie-based sessions (generic)”

For any site that uses session cookies without a framework like NextAuth:

  1. Walk through the login in Playwright.
  2. Extract cookies: cookies { domain: ".example.com" } — confirm which cookies are session-bearing.
  3. Declare connection(..., client="browser", auth={"type": "cookies", "domain": ".example.com"}). The engine’s brave-browser / firefox cookie providers seed the ambient Jar from the user’s real logged-in browser on the first call; the Jar persists rotated session tokens back to the vault via the __cookie_delta__ sidecar at tool exit.

Reference implementations:

  • apps/ai/claude/claude_web.py (Cloudflare-protected, client="fetch" + http2=False)
  • apps/logistics/amazon/amazon.py (tiered cookie architecture, Siege bypass via skip_cookies)

Tiered cookie architectures

Section titled “Tiered cookie architectures”

Large platforms like Amazon use multiple cookie tiers for different access levels:

TierCookiesAccess
Sessionsession-id, session-token, ubid-mainBrowsing, search
Persistencex-main”Remember me” across sessions
Authenticationat-main (Atza|...), sess-at-mainAccount pages, order history
SSOsst-main (Sst1|...), sso-state-mainCross-domain auth

When building an app against a tiered platform, you need the full cookie jar from a logged-in browser — not just the session cookie. The auth tokens are interdependent and the server validates them together.

Some cookies should be excluded (see 1-transport for cookie stripping) — encryption trigger cookies, WAF telemetry, etc. But the auth-tier cookies must all be present.

Auth boundaries

Section titled “Auth boundaries”

Not every operation needs a login. During discovery, classify each endpoint:

TierDescriptionExample
PublicWorks with just a frontend API keyGoodreads search, Exa search API
Suggested authRicher results with a session, but works withoutGoodreads reviews (adds viewerHasLiked)
Required authFails without session cookiesDashboard APIs, mutations, user-specific data

To map boundaries: send each request without auth. If you get data, it’s public. If you get partial data with errors on some fields, it’s suggested auth. If you get a 401/403 or an auth error, it’s required.

In the app’s readme, mark public tools with auth: none:

Runtime config discovery

Section titled “Runtime config discovery”

Some platforms rotate API keys or endpoints when they deploy. For these, build a multi-tier discovery chain that self-heals:

Note: File-based caching has been replaced by sandbox storage — the executor reads/writes cache vals on the app’s graph node. See Data & Storage.

Implementation

Section titled “Implementation”

Multi-environment bundles

Section titled “Multi-environment bundles”

Production JS bundles often ship configs for all environments. Pick Prod:

SignalExample
shortName field"shortName": "Prod"
Ads enabled"showAds": true
Analytics enabled"publishWebVitalMetrics": true

Reference: apps/media/goodreads/public_graph.py discover_from_bundle().

Examples

Section titled “Examples”
AppPatternWhat to learn from it
apps/logistics/amazon/Tiered cookie auth, Siege encryption bypass, SESSION_EXPIRED retryFull client hints, cookie stripping for anti-bot, session warming, provider retry convention
apps/web/exa/NextAuth email code → fully HTTPX (no browser) → API keysJS bundle scanning for custom endpoints, Navigation API interception, OTP token format discovery, Vercel http2=False bypass
apps/media/goodreads/Multi-tier discovery, AppSync, auth boundary mappingBundle extraction, config rotation, public vs auth operations
apps/ai/claude/Cloudflare-protected cookie extractionStealth Playwright settings, HttpOnly cookies via CDP
apps/fitness/austin-boulder-project/Bundle-extracted API key, tenant namespaceJS config scanning, namespace-as-auth

Vendor guides

Section titled “Vendor guides”
GuideWhen to read it
nextauth.mdSites with /api/auth/* endpoints, next-auth.* cookies
workos.mdSites with workos_id in JWT claims, WorkOS session IDs
macos-keychain.mdNative macOS apps, Electron Safe Storage, Google OAuth tokens, full credential audit
